cards.front: What is the difference between a vector's length and its capacity?
cards.back: Length is the number of elements currently in use. Capacity is the total amount of storage allocated. Capacity is always >= length.

cards.text: To get a vector's length, use {{c1::size()}}. To get its capacity, use {{c2::capacity()}}.
To add an element to the end of a vector, use {{c1::push_back()}} or {{c1::emplace_back()}}.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: Started with 3 elements, added 2 more with push_back. Total: 5 elements.

Vectors allocate more memory than needed to avoid frequent reallocations:
  • Reallocation requires copying all elements to new memory (expensive)
  • Typically doubles capacity when full (geometric growth)
  • Amortizes cost over many insertions
  • Trade-off: uses more memory for better performance

cards.text: To change a vector's length, use {{c1::resize()}}. To preallocate capacity without changing length, use {{c2::reserve()}}.

cards.front: What is the difference between resize() and reserve()?
cards.back:
resize(n) changes the length to n, adding/removing elements as needed.
reserve(n) preallocates capacity to n without changing length. Use reserve() when you know how many elements you'll add.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: resize(5) adds 2 new value-initialized elements (zeros). Size becomes 5, and v[4] is 0.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: reserve() only changes capacity, not size. Size remains 0, capacity is at least 100.

cards.front: When should you use reserve() with a vector?
cards.back: When you know approximately how many elements you'll add. This prevents multiple reallocations, improving performance. Especially useful before a loop that adds many elements.

cards.text: To reduce a vector's capacity to match its size, use {{c1::shrink_to_fit()}}. This is a request, not a guarantee.

cards.front: What happens when you push_back to a vector that's at capacity?
cards.back: The vector reallocates: allocates new memory (typically double the old capacity), copies all elements to new location, deletes old memory. This is expensive but happens infrequently due to geometric growth.
